title: Eigenvalues, Diagonalization, and Jordan Form

learningObjectives.description: Find eigenvalues by solving the characteristic equation and compute corresponding eigenvectors
Diagonalize a matrix as A = P Lambda P-inv when sufficient linearly independent eigenvectors exist
Apply eigendecomposition to compute matrix powers A^k = P Lambda^k P-inv efficiently
Explain Jordan canonical form and identify when a matrix is not diagonalisable
Connect the eigenspectrum to convergence of gradient descent and power iteration: faster convergence when the spectral gap is large
Relate eigendecomposition of A^T A to the SVD and explain why singular values are non-negative square roots of eigenvalues

content:
## Similarity and Diagonal Representations

A transformation $t: V \to V$ can look complicated in one basis and simple in another. The best possible representation is **diagonal** — where the matrix has nonzero entries only on the main diagonal. A diagonal transformation just scales each basis vector independently:

$$t(\vec{\beta}_i) = \lambda_i \vec{\beta}_i$$

When can we find such a basis? The vectors $\vec{\beta}_i$ must be sent to scalar multiples of themselves — they are **eigenvectors**, and $\lambda_i$ are **eigenvalues**.

## Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

For a transformation $t: V \to V$, a nonzero vector $\vec{v}$ is an **eigenvector** with **eigenvalue** $\lambda$ if:

$$t(\vec{v}) = \lambda\vec{v}$$

In matrix form: $A\vec{v} = \lambda\vec{v}$, or equivalently $(A - \lambda I)\vec{v} = \vec{0}$. For a nonzero $\vec{v}$ to exist, $A - \lambda I$ must be singular:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This is the **characteristic equation**. Expanding the determinant gives the **characteristic polynomial** $p(\lambda)$ — a degree-$n$ polynomial whose roots are the eigenvalues.

### Finding Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

1. Compute $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$ and find its roots $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_k$
2. For each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$, solve $(A - \lambda_i I)\vec{v} = \vec{0}$ by row reduction. The null space $\mathcal{N}(A - \lambda_i I)$ is the **eigenspace** of $\lambda_i$ — all eigenvectors for that eigenvalue plus $\vec{0}$.

### Properties

- $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_i \lambda_i$ (sum of eigenvalues = trace)
- $\det(A) = \prod_i \lambda_i$ (product of eigenvalues = determinant)
- Eigenvectors for **distinct** eigenvalues are linearly independent
- Real symmetric matrices always have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors

## Diagonalizability

An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is **diagonalizable** if it is similar to a diagonal matrix:

$$A = P\Lambda P^{-1}$$

where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$ and the columns of $P$ are the corresponding eigenvectors.

**When is $A$ diagonalizable?**
- $A$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors (equivalently: the eigenvectors span $\mathbb{R}^n$)
- $A$ has $n$ distinct eigenvalues → automatically diagonalizable
- For repeated eigenvalues: $A$ is diagonalizable iff the geometric multiplicity (dimension of the eigenspace) equals the algebraic multiplicity (multiplicity of the root in $p(\lambda)$) for every eigenvalue

### Powers and Functions of a Matrix

Diagonalization makes computing powers trivial:

$$A^k = P\Lambda^k P^{-1}, \qquad \Lambda^k = \text{diag}(\lambda_1^k, \ldots, \lambda_n^k)$$

Matrix exponential: $e^A = P\,\text{diag}(e^{\lambda_1}, \ldots, e^{\lambda_n})\,P^{-1}$. This underlies the solution of linear differential equations $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x}$.

## The Jordan Canonical Form

Not all matrices are diagonalizable. The next best thing is the **Jordan canonical form** — a block-diagonal matrix where each block corresponds to one eigenvalue:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} J_{n_1}(\lambda_1) & & \\ & \ddots & \\ & & J_{n_k}(\lambda_k) \end{bmatrix}$$

A **Jordan block** $J_m(\lambda)$ of size $m$ is:

$$J_m(\lambda) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ & & \ddots & \ddots & \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \lambda & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \lambda \end{bmatrix}$$

Diagonal blocks of size 1 are just the eigenvalue — a diagonalizable matrix has all $1 \times 1$ Jordan blocks. A block of size $> 1$ arises when there are fewer independent eigenvectors than the algebraic multiplicity of the eigenvalue.

**Theorem**: every square matrix over $\mathbb{C}$ (or over $\mathbb{R}$ if we allow complex eigenvectors) is similar to a Jordan canonical form. The Jordan form is unique up to reordering of blocks.

## Nilpotent Matrices

A matrix $N$ is **nilpotent** if $N^k = 0$ for some $k$. The only eigenvalue of a nilpotent matrix is 0. Every Jordan block with eigenvalue 0 is a nilpotent block; any nilpotent matrix is similar to a block of such blocks.

The Jordan form decomposes any transformation as:

$$A = D + N, \quad DN = ND$$

where $D$ is diagonalizable (semisimple part) and $N$ is nilpotent. This is the Jordan-Chevalley decomposition — the foundation of the theory of matrix functions and linear ODEs.

## Connection to Spectral Theory in ML

For **symmetric** matrices (which covariance matrices always are), the Jordan form is always diagonal with real entries — this is the spectral theorem. The eigendecomposition $A = Q\Lambda Q'$ always exists, with $Q$ orthogonal.

This is why PCA is tractable: the covariance matrix $X'X/(n-1)$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, so it always diagonalizes with non-negative real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. The Jordan form (with its potential for non-diagonal blocks) never arises in practice for covariance matrices.

terms.name: Characteristic equation
terms.desc: The condition for λ to be an eigenvalue. A − λI must be singular so that the homogeneous system (A − λI)v = 0 has a nonzero solution. Expanding the determinant gives the characteristic polynomial.

terms.name: Characteristic polynomial
terms.desc: A degree-n polynomial in λ. Its roots are the eigenvalues. The coefficient of λⁿ is (−1)ⁿ; the constant term is det(A); the coefficient of λⁿ⁻¹ involves the trace.

terms.name: Eigendecomposition
terms.desc: Valid when A has n linearly independent eigenvectors. Λ is diagonal with eigenvalues; P has eigenvectors as columns. Represents: change to eigenbasis (P⁻¹), scale each axis (Λ), change back (P).

terms.name: Matrix power via diagonalization
terms.desc: Diagonalizing trivializes powers because Λᵏ just raises each diagonal entry to the k-th power. This is why diagonalizability is so useful for dynamical systems and iterative algorithms.

terms.name: Jordan block of size m
terms.desc: An m×m matrix with λ on the diagonal and 1s on the superdiagonal. Represents a 'generalized' eigenvector chain: applying (A−λI) shifts along the chain until it annihilates after m steps.

terms.name: Defective eigenvalue
terms.desc: When a Jordan block has size > 1, the eigenvalue is defective: its geometric multiplicity (number of independent eigenvectors = 1) is less than its algebraic multiplicity (size of block = m). Diagonalization fails for this eigenvalue.

references.label: Hefferon 2020
references.title: Linear Algebra, Ch. Five: Similarity, Eigenvalues, Jordan Form
